title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, caste, color, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct applies within community spaces, also applies individual officially representing community public spaces. Examples representing community include using official e-mail address, posting via official social media account, acting appointed representative online offline event.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Instances abusive, harassing, otherwise unacceptable behavior may reported community leaders responsible enforcement luke-jenkins-dev@outlook.com. complaints reviewed investigated promptly fairly. community leaders obligated respect privacy security reporter incident.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders follow Community Impact Guidelines determining consequences action deem violation Code Conduct:

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Use inappropriate language behavior deemed unprofessional unwelcome community. Consequence: private, written warning community leaders, providing clarity around nature violation explanation behavior inappropriate. public apology may requested.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: violation single incident series actions. Consequence: warning consequences continued behavior. interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, specified period time. includes avoiding interactions community spaces well external channels like social media. Violating terms may lead temporary permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: serious violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior. Consequence: temporary ban sort interaction public communication community specified period time. public private interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, allowed period. Violating terms may lead permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Demonstrating pattern violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior, harassment individual, aggression toward disparagement classes individuals. Consequence: permanent ban sort public interaction within community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct adapted Contributor Covenant, version 2.1, available https://www.contributor-covenant.org/version/2/1/code_of_conduct.html. Community Impact Guidelines inspired [Mozilla’s code conduct enforcement ladder][https://github.com/mozilla/inclusion]. answers common questions code conduct, see FAQ https://www.contributor-covenant.org/faq. Translations available https://www.contributor-covenant.org/translations.

what: Schema-Based Validation of R Objects With User-Defined Rules

text: R Validator validation data user-defined schemas. package development subject change constantly.

title: Schema-Based Validation of R Objects With User-Defined Rules
text: can install development version RV like :

title: Schema-Based Validation of R Objects With User-Defined Rules
text: RV provides three S7 classes: Registry, Schema, Validator. Registry defines rules stores built-RV rule names definitions. Schema takes user-defined schema, validates schema, reorders schema according order defined Registry. default Schema creates Registry one passed function. Validator takes data user-defined Schema, applies Schema field data. three passes, first applying control rules, transform rules, validate rules. list given schema passed Schema() ingest.
code: library(RV) r <- Registry()  S7::prop_names(r) #>  [1] "rule_names"          "control_rules"       "transform_rules"     #>  [4] "validate_rules"      "str_to_fn_rules"     "str_to_fn_converter" #>  [7] "type_names"          "type_map"            "coerce_names"        #> [10] "coerce_map"          "schema_rules"        "cross_rule_names"    #> [13] "cross_rules"         "validator_rules" s <- Schema(list(type = "integer", default = 1L))  s@schema #> $default #> [1] 1 #>  #> $type #> [1] "integer"  S7::prop_names(s) #> [1] "schema"           "errors"           "Registry"         ".schema_cache"    #> [5] "error"            "error_print_opts" "valid" v <- Validator(   data = list(a = 1, b = "Hello"),   schema = list(     a = list(       type = "numeric",       min_val = 0,       max_val = 5     ),     b = list(       type = "character",       apply = "\\(x) paste(x, 'World!')"     ),     c = list(       required = FALSE,       type = "data.frame"     ),     d = list(       default = 10L     )   ) )  v@data #> $a #> [1] 1 #>  #> $b #> [1] "Hello World!" #>  #> $d #> [1] 10  S7::prop_names(v) #> [1] "data"             "Schema"           "errors"           ".validator_cache" #> [5] "error"            "valid"

title: Schema-Based Validation of R Objects With User-Defined Rules
text: RV schemas lists rule-named leaf elements specify behaviour validator. list structure determines matching data object. Non-leaf elements can named unnamed, name-matching attempted first matching position. 2 key points remember creating schemas: schema node operates 1 level ‘’ applied data. Rule elements considered matching position (e.g., first non-rule element matched position/index 1). See following example rule designed fail: able named unnamed schema elements allows flexibility, must used care: Schemas duplicate names depth, RV check input data, therefore normal R rules apply (first name-match prevails):
code: Validator(   data = list(1.5, 5L, a = list(10L)),   schema = list(     # this rule is at the top level, meaning it will operate '1 level above',     # aka on the entire 'data' object.     type = "numeric",      # for 'data[[1]]', the schema field will be 'schema[[1]][[1]]'     # this schema field is treated as index 1 as the element prior was a rule     # so therefore is not included in matching.     list(type = "integer"),      # name matching disregards position in the node, so this will match to 'a'.     # again, the '1 level above' means to test the value within the 'a' list, we     # need to use 'list()' again to specify we're applying a rule to the     # value at position 1.     a = list(list(type = "double"))   ) )@errors #> $type #> [1] "Is not type `numeric`." #>  #> [[2]] #> [[2]]$type #> [1] "Is not type `integer`." #>  #>  #> $a #> $a[[1]] #> $a[[1]]$type #> [1] "Is not type `double`." Validator(   data = list(a = 1L),   schema = list(     list(type = "integer"), # this matches to 'data[[1]]', which is 'a'.     a = list(type = "character") # this also matches to 'a' as same names!   ) )@errors #> [[1]] #> [[1]]$type #> NULL #>  #>  #> $a #> $a$type #> [1] "Is not type `character`." # duplicate names at same level (also invalid for duplicate rules) Schema(list(   a = list(type = "integer"),   a = list(type = "character") ))@valid #> [1] FALSE  # duplicate names in data, 1st match prevails Validator(   data = list(a = 1L, a = "Hello"),   schema = list(     a = list(type = "character")   ) )@errors #> $a #> $a$type #> [1] "Is not type `character`."

title: Schema-Based Validation of R Objects With User-Defined Rules
text: add rules RV, use add_rule variants:
code: data <- structure(1L, my_attr = "Hi")  mySchema <- Schema(list(check_my_attr = 1L)) mySchema@errors #> $check_my_attr #> [1] "Unknown rule: `check_my_attr`."  mySchema <- add_rule(   obj = mySchema,   name = "check_my_attr",   validator_fn = function(data_field, schema_field, ...) {     if (attr(data_field, "my_attr") != schema_field) {       list(error = "Data doesn't match schema 'my_attr'.")     }   },   schema_fn = function(schema_field, ...) {     if (!is.character(schema_field) || length(schema_field) != 1L) {       "Must be length 1 character"     }   },   rule_type = "validate" )  mySchema@errors #> $check_my_attr #> [1] "Must be length 1 character"  mySchema@schema$check_my_attr <- "Hi" Validator(data, mySchema)@valid #> [1] TRUE

what: RV: Schema-Based Validation of R Objects With User-Defined Rules — RV-package
title: RV: Schema-Based Validation of R Objects With User-Defined Rules — RV-package
text: schema-based validation framework R objects using user-defined rules. Provides three S7 classes 'Registry', 'Schema', 'Validator' manage rules, define list-based schemas, validate data flexible extensible manner.

title: RV: Schema-Based Validation of R Objects With User-Defined Rules — RV-package
text: Maintainer: Luke Jenkins luke-jenkins-dev@outlook.com (ORCID) [copyright holder]

title: Create a Registry Object — Registry
text: S7 class defines rules validating data Schema.

title: Create a Registry Object — Registry
text: x object tested.

title: Create a Registry Object — Registry
text: S7 Registry object following properties: rule_names rule names, order evaluation. Derived control/transform/validate rules apply_last added end. 'control_rules Rules influence control flow. applied first pass validating data. 'transform_rules' Rules transform data. applied second pass validating data. 'validate_rules' Rules validate data. applied penultimate pass validating (last pass special apply_last pass - see details). str_to_fn_rules Schema rules allowed string function values, string values converted functions automatically schema validation. str_to_fn_converter function converts string values functions str_to_fn_rules. type_names character vector allowed type names, derived keys type_map environment. type_map environment mapping type names type definition functions. coerce_names character vector allowed coercion rule names, derived keys coerce_map environment. coerce_map environment mapping type names coercion functions. rule_names character vector (non-cross) rule names. schema_rules environment containing schema rule functions. cross_rule_names character vector allowed schema cross rule names, derived keys cross_rules environment. cross_rules environment containing schema cross rule functions (rules check relationships multiple schema rule values). validator_rules environment containing validator rule functions.

title: Create a Registry Object — Registry
text: Registry class serves central repository rules used schema data validation RV based workflows. includes built-rules common validation tasks allows addition custom rules. Registry objects automatically created Schema objects (passed Validator objects), rules can added registry directly within objects using rule-adding generic functions. therefore necessary create Registry object separately one wish. However, Schema Validator objects automatically re-validated rules added, addition many new rules, beneficial create Registry object, add rules , pass classes.

title: Create a Registry Object — Registry
code: r <- Registry() s <- Schema(list(type = "integer"), registry = r)  is.Registry(r) #> [1] TRUE

title: Create a Schema Object — Schema
text: S7 class defines validates schema future validation R objects , using Validator.

title: Create a Schema Object — Schema
code: Schema(schema, registry = Registry(), error = FALSE, ...)  is.Schema(x)

title: Create a Schema Object — Schema
text: schema non-empty list defining schema. leaf element validated must named rules registered schema rule registry (see Registry add_rule). Named elements must unique given level list. registry object class Registry containing schema validator rules use schema validation. provided, default registry built-rules used. error single logical value. TRUE, constructor throws error schema invalid. ... named arguments passed internal error message formatting functions: max_depth, max_width, max_rows, UTF8 control truncation printing error message schema validation fails. max_ arguments accept integerish scalar values, UTF8 accepts single logical value. Unnamed arguments names ignored. Stored error_print_opts property. x object tested.

title: Create a Schema Object — Schema
text: S7 Schema object following properties: schema input schema list, strings converted functions applicable. errors (Read ) list mirroring schema structure, NULL valid rules character error messages invalid ones. Registry object class Registry containing schema validator rules used validation. .schema_cache (Internal, read ) environment caching schema validation results. error Boolean; whether error invalid schemas. error_print_opts list options error message printing error = TRUE. Options max_depth, max_width, max_rows, UTF8, control truncation printing error message validation fails. options also used Validator class ingests Schema. valid (Read ) Boolean; TRUE schema valid, FALSE otherwise.

title: Create a Schema Object — Schema
text: element input list represents field named rules (e.g., 'type', 'required', 'min_val') checked schema rule registry. Cross rules (e.g., 'min_val_larger_than_max_val', 'min_val' < 'max_val') also evaluated constituent rules pass individually. schema re-evaluated upon change schema properties, @valid property indicates whether schema valid (rules pass) invalid (rule fails). invalid, passed Validator validation fail immediately. See Registry class details available rules customise . See Validator class details use Schema validate data.

title: Create a Schema Object — Schema
code: # A valid schema s <- Schema(list(   name = list(type = "character", required = TRUE),   age = list(type = "numeric", min_val = 0, max_val = 150) )) s@valid # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  # An invalid schema (type must be a string) s <- Schema(list(name = list(type = 123))) s@valid # FALSE #> [1] FALSE s@errors # error message #> $name #> $name$type #> [1] "Must be a function or a string." #>  #>   # To error on invalid schema try(Schema(list(name = list(type = 123)), error = TRUE)) #> Error : <RV::Schema> object is invalid: #> - Schema validation failed with the following errors: #> └─ name #>   └─ type: Must be a function or a string.

title: Create a Validator Object — Validator
text: S7 class validates R objects Schema. Validator takes schema (object class Schema list can converted Schema) validates input data rules defined schema.

title: Create a Validator Object — Validator
text: data non-empty list, data.frame, atomic vector validated schema. schema Schema object non-empty list defining schema. See Schema constructor details schema structure rules. error single logical value. TRUE, error thrown validation fails. x object tested.

title: Create a Validator Object — Validator
text: S7 Validator object following properties: data validated input data, changes made validation. Schema object class Schema. also contains Registry used validation, can accessed via @Schema@Registry. errors (Read ) list mirroring data schema structure (depending match_schema match_data), NULL valid fields character error messages invalid ones. Extra fields showing missigness may present match_schema match_data TRUE. .validator_cache (Internal, read ) environment caching validation results. error Boolean; whether error upon failure (invalid data schema). valid (Read ) Boolean; TRUE schema valid data validation rules pass, FALSE otherwise (invalid data schema).

title: Create a Validator Object — Validator
text: validation process checks field schema data, creating errors list mirrors schema structure NULL valid fields character error messages invalid fields. validation results stored @errors property, overall validity indicated @valid property. @error property/argument set TRUE, validation failure result error thrown. customise truncation error messages, see @Schema@error_print_opts property. Validator re-evaluated upon change object properties. invalid schema provided, validation fail immediately validation error indicating schema invalid. see errors schema validation, see Validator@Schema@errors property. See Schema class details schema class structure, Registry class details available validation rules.

title: Create a Validator Object — Validator
code: v <- Validator(   data = list(name = "Alice", age = 30),   schema = list(     name = list(type = "character", required = TRUE),     age = list(type = "numeric", min_val = 0, max_val = 150)   ) ) v@valid # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  # Schema object can be given directly s <- Schema(list(a = list(type = "numeric"), b = list(type = "character"))) v <- Validator(list("Hello", 42), s) v@valid # FALSE #> [1] FALSE v@errors #> $a #> $a$type #> [1] "No data for field." #>  #>  #> $b #> $b$type #> [1] "No data for field." #>  #>   # To error on invalid schema or data try(Validator(list("Hello", 42), s, error = TRUE)) #> Error : <RV::Validator> object is invalid: #> - Data validation failed with the following errors: #> ├─ a #> │ └─ type: No data for field. #> └─ b #>   └─ type: No data for field.  # Invalid schemas show their errors try(Validator(list(42), list(type = 123), error = TRUE)) #> Error : <RV::Validator> object is invalid: #> - Schema validation failed with the following errors: #> └─ type: Must be a function or a string.

what: Add rules to a Regsitry — add_rule
title: Add rules to a Regsitry — add_rule
text: Add validator, schema, (schema) cross, type, coerce rules Registry, Schema, Validator object.

title: Add rules to a Regsitry — add_rule
code: add_rule(   obj,   name,   validator_fn,   schema_fn = NULL,   rule_type = c("validate", "control", "transform") )  add_cross_rule(obj, name, rule_names, cross_fn)  add_type_rule(obj, type_name, type_fn)  add_coerce_rule(obj, coerce_name, coerce_fn)

title: Add rules to a Regsitry — add_rule
text: obj Registry, Schema, Validator object. name string specifying name rule add. Rule names existing rule names. validator_fn function validates data field schema field. Every validator rule function must return NULL named list. NULL return indicates validation success transformed data alteration control flow. named list return can accept following elements: data: (optionally transformed) data value reassigned passed subsequent rules. returned, original data value used. error: string error message validation fails. returned, validation assumed succeeded. continue: boolean indicating whether continue validating subsequent rules. returned, assumed TRUE. schema_fn function validate schema field definition. NULL, default function performs validation used. Schema validation functions return NULL schema field valid, string error message invalid. rule_type string specifying type rule add. Must one "control", "transform", "validate". rule_names character vector specifying names existing schema rules cross rule operate . Must length 2 , names must existing schema rules Registry. cross_fn function validate schema field definitions across multiple schema rules. Schema cross functions return NULL schema fields valid, string error message invalid. type_name, coerce_name string specifying name type/coerce rule value add. type_fn, coerce_fn function validate/coerce field value.

title: Add rules to a Regsitry — add_rule
text: Invisibly returns input object new rule added.

title: Add rules to a Regsitry — add_rule
text: able correctly pass required arguments data/schema validations, rule functions must following argument semantics: Note: field schema_field positional, thus can called anything, whilst .self (object : Schema schema rules, Validator validator rules) .data (full data walk) named arguments must named . type/coerce rule functions: function(field) schema/cross rule functions: function(field, ...) function(field, .self, ...) | function(field, .data, ...) function(field, .self, .data) validator rule functions: function(field, schema_field, ...) function(field, schema_field, .self, ...) | function(field, schema_field, .data, ...) function(field, schema_field, .self, .data) Registry uses environments store rules, mutable, add_rule methods copy existing environment new rule new environment, meaning original object modified. See examples.

title: Add rules to a Regsitry — add_rule
code: mySchema <- Schema(list(check_my_attr = 1L)) mySchema@errors # doesn't recognise rule #> $check_my_attr #> [1] "Unknown rule: `check_my_attr`." #>   mySchema <- add_rule(   obj = mySchema,   name = "check_my_attr",   validator_fn = function(data_field, schema_field, ...) {     if (attr(data_field, "my_attr") != schema_field) {       list(error = "Data doesn't match schema 'my_attr'.")     }   },   schema_fn = function(schema_field, ...) {     if (!is.character(schema_field) || length(schema_field) != 1L) {       "Must be length 1 character"     }   },   rule_type = "validate" )  # rule recognised and schema automatically re-validated mySchema@errors #> $check_my_attr #> [1] "Must be length 1 character" #>   # validation works with the new rule mySchema@schema$check_my_attr <- "Hi" Validator(structure(1L, my_attr = "Hi"), mySchema)@valid # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  # schema cross rules invalidate when constituent rules are invalid mySchema <- add_cross_rule(   obj = mySchema,   name = "min_and_max_val_add_to_10",   rule_names = c("min_val", "max_val"),   cross_fn = function(schema_field, ...) {     if (schema_field$min_val + schema_field$max_val == 10) {       "min_val and max_val cannot add to 10."     }   } ) mySchema@schema <- list(min_val = 2, max_val = 8) mySchema@errors # cross rule error #> $min_val #> [1] "min_val and max_val cannot add to 10." #>  #> $max_val #> [1] "min_val and max_val cannot add to 10." #>   r <- Registry() r <- add_type_rule(r, "my_type", function(x) {   inherits(x, "my_type") }) s <- Schema(list(type = "my_type"), registry = r) s@valid # TRUE #> [1] TRUE Validator(structure(1, class = "my_type"), s)@valid # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  r <- add_coerce_rule(r, "my_type", function(x) {   structure(x, class = c("my_type", class(x))) }) s <- Schema(list(coerce = "my_type"), registry = r) v <- Validator(1, s) class(v@data) # "my_type" "numeric" #> [1] "my_type" "numeric"  # environments are copied, so original registry is not modified r <- Registry() r2 <- add_type_rule(r, "my_type", function(x) x) identical(r@type_map, r2@type_map) # FALSE #> [1] FALSE
